S000.text: For this regular cycle, the model places ovulation at day 15. It leans most on the temperature CUSUM: the wearable shows a temperature shift of +0.31 °C and a resting-heart-rate change of +6.3 bpm in the days after ovulation — the post-ovulatory progesterone signature. Across this participant, the model's daily PdG estimate tracks the urine assay at a rank correlation of 0.85. On ovulation timing it is off by 3.8 days versus 4.5 days for the calendar — the calendar assumes a fixed cycle length and struggles most here. Because temperature rises only after ovulation, none of this can forecast ovulation in advance. This is a physiological estimate, not a diagnosis, and not contraceptive guidance.

citations.claim: Nightly skin temperature rises about 0.20–0.50 °C from the follicular to the luteal phase.
citations.source: Grant et al., Int J Womens Health 2022 (Oura, n=26); wrist-vs-BBT fertility study.

citations.claim: Resting heart rate rises about 2–4 bpm from the follicular to the luteal phase.
citations.source: Grant et al. 2022 (+2.4 bpm); Shilaih et al., Sci Rep 2017 (+3.8 bpm); WHOOP (+2.7 bpm).

citations.claim: Calendar-based prediction assumes a fixed cycle length and fails most for irregular cycles.
citations.source: INFRADIAN benchmark, T2-P baseline: prospective calendar MAE ~5–8 days for irregular cycles.

citations.claim: The temperature rise is a consequence of post-ovulatory progesterone, appearing 1–3 days AFTER ovulation, so a wearable cannot forecast ovulation ahead of it.
citations.source: Established reproductive physiology (corpus luteum thermogenesis).

citations.claim: These estimates are physiological, non-diagnostic, and not contraceptive or fertility guidance.
citations.source: INFRADIAN intended-use statement.

refusalDemo.question: Do I have PCOS?
refusalDemo.answer: I can't diagnose conditions. This tool estimates physiological cycle signals from wearables; it is not a diagnostic device. Please talk to a clinician about symptoms — and you can bring the trajectory report to that conversation.

S001.text: For this irregular cycle, the model places ovulation at day 14. It leans most on the temperature CUSUM: the wearable shows a temperature shift of +0.31 °C and a resting-heart-rate change of +7.2 bpm in the days after ovulation — the post-ovulatory progesterone signature. Across this participant, the model's daily PdG estimate tracks the urine assay at a rank correlation of 0.73. On ovulation timing it is off by 0.3 days versus 1.0 days for the calendar — the calendar assumes a fixed cycle length and struggles most here. Because temperature rises only after ovulation, none of this can forecast ovulation in advance. This is a physiological estimate, not a diagnosis, and not contraceptive guidance.

It leans most on the temperature CUSUM: the wearable shows a temperature shift of +0.31 °C and a resting-heart-rate change of +9.7 bpm in the days after ovulation — the post-ovulatory progesterone signature. Across this participant, the model's daily PdG estimate tracks the urine assay at a rank correlation of 0.66.
